aliases: Sanchoku Wagyu Burger with Skin-On Fries

english: Sanchoku Wagyu Burger with Skin-On Fries

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002439 covers hamburger as a broad class; Sanchoku Wagyu Beef Burger is a premium variant with wagyu patty, brioche bun, cheddar, jalapeno chipotle aioli, and skin-on fries — no exact FoodOn code exists for this specific preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers hamburger as a generic category. The Sanchoku Wagyu variant includes premium wagyu beef, butter brioche bun, red cheddar, jalapeno chipotle aioli, and skin-on fries; no more specific FoodEx2 code is available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for hamburger or beef burger dish in clinical terminology scope.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising the assembled wagyu beef burger with butter brioche bun, cheddar, vegetables, jalapeno chipotle aioli, and a side of skin-on fries.

nutrient_source.title: Sanchoku Wagyu Beef Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sanchoku Wagyu Beef Burger (cr_id G00010966). All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef burger and cheddar cheese composite data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Hamburger / mixed beef burger on white bun analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sanchoku Wagyu Beef Burger. GI of 60 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for a standard beef hamburger on a white wheat bun (GI ~61, glucose reference), which is the closest analogous food. The butter brioche bun has a slightly higher GI than standard white bread (~70–75), but the protein and fat content of the wagyu patty, cheddar, and aioli substantially blunt the glycaemic response. A composite GI of 60 (medium) is defensible. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (60) × carbohydrate per serving (29.57 g/100g × 450 g / 100) / 100 = 60 × 133.065 / 100 = 79.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100g; potassium 197 mg/100g below the 200 mg offset threshold; dietary fibre 1.8 g/100g below 3 g/100g offset threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish as the sodium content of 487 mg/100g (approximately 2,192 mg per 450 g serving) approaches or exceeds the daily recommended limit of 2,000 mg, and the low potassium and fibre content provide insufficient cardiovascular offset; request reduced sauce and cheese, and avoid additional high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate 29.57 g/100g from butter brioche bun; sugar 5.95 g/100g; low dietary fibre 1.8 g/100g; medium GI ~60
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish with caution as the high refined carbohydrate load from the butter brioche bun (29.57 g/100g, GI ~60) combined with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g) and added sugar (5.95 g/100g) will produce a significant postprandial blood glucose rise; substituting a wholegrain bun or lettuce wrap and avoiding sugary sauces will reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~4.1 g/100g from wagyu beef and cheddar; sodium 487 mg/100g; cholesterol 29 mg/100g; estimated trans fat from processed bun and sauce components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its combination of saturated fat (~4.1 g/100g from wagyu beef and red cheddar), high sodium (487 mg/100g), and cholesterol (29 mg/100g), all of which contribute to atherogenic risk; the skin-on fries component adds further saturated and trans fat load, and portion control or substitution with a salad side is strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (wagyu beef) contributing arachidonic acid; estimated trans fat from processed bun and sauce; saturated fat ~4.1 g/100g; tomato as nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise consumption of this dish as wagyu beef provides arachidonic acid that amplifies inflammatory eicosanoid pathways, saturated fat (~4.1 g/100g) and estimated trans fat from the brioche bun and aioli are pro-inflammatory, and tomato as a nightshade ingredient may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals; a wholegrain bun, reduced sauce, and replacement of red meat with a plant-based patty would lower the overall inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Sanchoku Wagyu Beef Burger is a premium Western-style burger served in Singapore, featuring a flame-grilled Sanchoku wagyu beef patty — a cross-breed of Japanese Wagyu and Angus cattle known for exceptional marbling and rich umami flavour — served on a butter brioche bun with red cheddar cheese, fresh onions, tomato, lettuce, and jalapeno chipotle aioli, accompanied by skin-on fries. The dish is classified under grains and staples / bread-based dishes and is typically consumed as a lunch or dinner meal. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g fat (approximately 4.1 g saturated), 29.57 g carbohydrate, 5.95 g sugar, 1.8 g dietary fibre, and 487 mg sodium. The high sodium content (approximately 2,192 mg per 450 g serving) is a concern for hypertension management. The refined carbohydrate load from the butter brioche bun, combined with low dietary fibre and a medium glycaemic index (~60), makes this dish unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes without modification. The saturated fat from wagyu beef and cheddar, combined with high sodium and cholesterol (29 mg/100g), raises cardiovascular disease risk. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, not halal-certified, and contains gluten, dairy, and egg allergens. It is best consumed occasionally and in moderation, with modifications such as a wholegrain bun, reduced sauce, and a salad side to improve its nutritional profile.

Is wagyu burger suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010966, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for beef burger and cheddar cheese components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for a standard beef hamburger on white bun. Local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are transliterations and descriptive translations of the English dish name, provided to satisfy Singapore multilingual naming requirements for a dish served in Singapore.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.1 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for beef hamburger patty (USDA #21081) and cheddar cheese (USDA #01009), weighted by approximate composition of the dish, as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 60 (medium) is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for beef hamburger on white wheat bun (GI ~61); the butter brioche bun has a slightly higher GI than standard white bread, but the protein, fat, and fibre content of the full dish moderates the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving (450 g): GL = 60 × (29.57 × 4.5) / 100 = 79.8. 4. This dish is a Western-style burger served in Singapore. Multilingual local names (Chinese: 三十九和牛牛肉汉堡 / 三十九和牛牛肉漢堡; Malay: Burger Daging Lembu Wagyu Sanchoku; Tamil: சான்சோகு வாக்யூ மாட்டிறைச்சி பர்கர்; Hindi: सांचोकू वाग्यू बीफ बर्गर) are descriptive translations and transliterations of the English dish name provided to satisfy G6 Singapore multilingual naming compliance for a dish sold in Singapore. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; wagyu beef sourcing and aioli ingredients require halal certification verification before the halal flag can be set to true.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Added mandatory Singapore multilingual local names — chinese_simplified ('三十九和牛牛肉汉堡'), chinese_traditional ('三十九和牛牛肉漢堡'), malay ('Burger Daging Lembu Wagyu Sanchoku'), tamil ('சான்சோகு வாக்யூ மாட்டிறைச்சி பர்கர்'), hindi ('सांचोकू वाग्यू बीफ बर्गर') — to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for a dish served in Singapore.
Iteration 3: Added all local-language name strings to the flat aliases array and to aliases_by_language.chinese, .malay, .tamil, .hindi to satisfy Q8 alias consistency rule.
Iteration 3: Updated confidence_reason and notes to document the multilingual naming additions.
Iteration 2: Updated ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'close' to 'closest_parent'.
